chapter:
Lesson-0 Welcome to (our) course!
Tamil has syllabic alphabets. A syllabic alphabet is an alphabet which has sounds always ending with a vowel, thus considered to be a syllable. Thus you have 12 vowels and 18 consonants.
VOWELS
These 12 vowels are crisp and sharp.
அ - pronounced is a short "a" , or to be more precise "u" in "uncle" ஆ- pronounced is a long "aa" , Example: "a" in "father" இ- pronounced as a short "i" , Example: "i" in "it" ஈ- pronounced as long "ii", Example: "Ee" in "bee" உ - pronounced as short "u", Example: "u" in "put" ஊ - pronounced as long "u", Example: "u" in "puma" எ - pronounced as short "e", Example: "e" in "let" ஈ- pronounced as long "e", Example: "a" in "ate" ஐ - pronounced as the english letter "I", Example: "eye" as in "eye" itsely ஒ- pronouced as short "o", Example: "o", Example: "o" in "poke" (not "ow" but rather a crisp "o") ஊ- pronounced as long "o", Example: "o", Example: "o" in "fort" ஔ- pronounced as "ow", as in "Powder"
I hope you found the sound of the vowels clear. Now let us see theconsonants.
There are 18 consonants, which are again crisp and clear.
க - KA as in "Karter" ங - NGA as in"English" ச - CHA as in "Chop" ஞ-GNA as in "ONION" ட - TA as in "TAILOR" (the t is not prounced on the teeth but a little back of the teeth) ண - NA (no english equivalent, try to pronounce "N" by curling your tongue) த -Tha (Theater) ந - na ப - pa ம -ma ய - ya ர - ra ல - la (dental l) வ - va ழ - ZHA (It is like the american "R" in "COurse") ள - la (l by curling the tongue) ற - (ra) ன - na
If you have clearly noticed, there are some consonants which may sound a little similar. You will see a much clear note once we get in further. For the time-being let us not think about "r", "n" and "l"
The remaining letters are: க - KA ச - CHa ட - TA த -Tha ப - pa ம -ma ய - ya வ - va
Special consonants
ஜ -ja ஷ - sha க்ஷ - ksha ஸ - sa ஹ - ha
Consonant + Vowel In Tamil, vowels are called as "Uyir" meaning "Life" and the consonants are called "Mey". They form together to represent other syllables such as ki, ku etc. This is know as Uyirmey Once you know how to write consonants, it is easy to write Uyirmey.
The consonant itself represents "Ka" as in "K+A" For, KAA simply add "" For, KI simply add "" For, KII simply add "" For, KE simply add "" For, KEE simply add "" For, KO simply add "" For, KOO simply add "" For, KAI simply add "" For, KOW simply add ""
The same rule is applicable for other consonants. But note that: As for "U" and "UU", there are some irregulars for pretty much all the consonants.For this, You can refer to the table below. For the consonant "ட", both "i" "ii" and "u" "uu" might seem irregular
???Insert a table here???
Exercise-1: Can you write the same for " ஞ, ட, ண, த, ந, ப, ம, ய, ர, ல, வ, ழ, ள, ற, ன ".
Note: For "NGA", Vowel combination is not necessary, as it occurs only as a combination with "K". We will see about it soon
Exercise-2: Can you write the following Tamil words written the Latin scipt in Tamil script? 1. VAA! - _____ 2. POO! -______ 3. KAATU - _____ 4. MALAI - _____ 5. KATHAVU- ______ 6. PUU -________
Note: Do not forget, that in this book , we are using "O" as short "O" and "OO" as long "O". Same for "U", "I", "E" and "A".
Did you notice how "THA" is pronounced as "DHA"? Well, In Tamil the har sounds are represented using the soft sounds itself. Though for writing it might seems easy, pay attention to the recording to distinguish the correct sound. The same is applicable to "KA" which is also "GA" , "TA" which is also "Da", "PA" which is also "BA", and "CHA" which is also "SA"
Exercise-3 Listen to the recording and see how these letters are pronounced. 1. படம் PATAM - பட்டு PATTU 2. நகம் NAKAM - கயிறு KAYIRU 3. பதவி PATHAVI - தண்ணீர் THANNIIR 4. புதுசு PUTHUSU - பயம் PAYAM 5. பசு PASU - பச்சை PACHCHAI Did you notice how "" is pronounces as ""? In Tamil, there is no difference between KA / GA CHA / SA TA/ DA THA/ DHA PA/BA But usually, there is a pattern, like where how it is pronounced.
The good thing, however, is that, when the consonant is doubled and followed by vowel, these five letters retain the original sound!
1.பாட்டு PAATTU- காடு KAADU 2. பச்சை PACHCHAI- மனசு MANASU 3. புத்தகம் PUTHTHAKAM- பாதி PAATHI 4. பக்கம் PAKKAM பாகம் PAAKAM 5. கப்பல் KAPPAL ஆபத்து AAPATHTHU
Lesson-1- வணக்கம் உங்கள் பெயர் என்ன ? - உங்க பேரு என்ன ? - Hello, What's your name?
In this unit, you'll learn to talk about yourself and your family.
Dialogue-1
Listen to the dialoge and repeat Standard tamil : வணக்கம்! உங்கள் பெயர் என்ன ? : என் பெயர் அருண், உங்கள் பெயர் என்ன ? : என் பெயர் ராம் . Colloquial tamil : ஹலோ, உங்க பேரு என்ன : எ(ன்) பேரு அருண், உங்க பேரு? : எ(ன்) பேரு ராம்
Pronounciation tip
Did you notice how the last consonant is usually dropped and how the last "n" is nasalaised? Well, That is one of the primary differences between Standard and colloquial tamil. Do not worry about the differences, soon you'll recognize the pattern easily.
என் - எ(ன்)- My உங்கள் - உங்க(ள்) - your பெயர்- பேரு - Name என்ன - என்ன What
Grammar
You may notice there is no "is/are" in the dialogue. In Tamil, if the Subject,i.e., I, she, his name needs a complement to satisfy , the "to be " verb is not necessary!
Dialogue-2
S.T: : எப்படி இருக்கிறீர்கள் : நான் நன்றாக இருக்கிறேன், நீங்கள்? : நானும் நன்றாக இருக்கிறேன்
C.T: : எப்படி இருக்கீங்க : நா(ன்) நல்லா இருக்கே(ன்), நீங்க? : நானு(ம்) நல்லா இருக்கே(ன்)
Pronounciation
Here, notice have "னும்" changes to "னு(ம்)"
Grammar
Here, When ___ says "I am fine", he uses the verb "am", which is "இருக்கிறேன்/ இருக்கே(ன்)". Well, it is used because the subject நான்/ நா(ன்) is indicating the state of the person. In tamil, people usually say "I am good"
Word order
In tamil, the word order is S-O-V. What does it mean? In English we say, I am fine. Here, I is the subject or the action-doer Am is the verb "to be" for I And then the object or the adverb I In Tamil, We always say "I fine am"
"The"
In english, we often use "the" to mention to a "particular" object. Whereas in Tamil, it doesn't exist.However, to precise something "this/ that " is used Example: Colombo is the capital of Srilanka. In tamil, you say S.T: Ilangayin thlainagaram Kozhumbu, C.T: Srilankavoda capital Colombo. (Literally, It means Srilanka's capital Colombo) Did you notice how almost all the words in colloquial tamil are in English? Well, some times Colloquial Tamil is referred to as "Tanglish", a mix of "Tamil" and "English". USually Technical words, countries name are used in colloquial language.
Vocabulary
நன்றாக நல்லா good இருக்கிறீர்கள் இருக்கீங்க are இருக்கிறேன் இருக்கே(ன்) am எப்படி எப்படி How
Here is a list of the pronouns and the verb "to be" நான் நா(ன்) I நீ நீ you (inf. sg) நீங்கள் நீங்க(ள்) you (f.sg) அவன் அவ(ன்) he (inf.sg) அவள் அவ(ள்) she (inf.sg) அது அது It/ that அவர் அவர் He/ She (f.sg)
நாம் நாம் We (we including the person spoken to) நாங்கள் நாங்க(ள்) We (we excluding the person spoken to) நீங்கள் நீங்க(ள்) you (inf. and f. pl) அவர்கள் அவங்க They
//Try to add this list in lesson -2 //lesso-1 ot over இருக்கிறேன் இருக்குறே(ன்) / இருக்கேன் am இருக்கிறாய் இருக்குற /இருக்க are இருக்கிறான் இருக்குறா(ன்) /இருக்கான் is இருக்கிறாள் இருக்குறா(ள்) /இருக்கா is இருக்கிறது இருக்குது /இருக்கு is
இருக்கிறோம் இருக்குறோ(ம்) /இருக்கோம் are இருக்கிறோம் இருக்குறோ(ம்) / இருக்கோம் are இருக்கிறீர்கள் இருக்குறீங்க / இருக்கீங்க are இருக்கிறார்கள் இருக்குறாங்க / இருக்காங்க are இருக்கின்றன இருக்குதுங்க / இருக்குங்க are
Dialogur 3:
: நீங்கள் எந்த ஊர் ? :திருச்சி . நீங்கள்? :நான் கன்னியாகுமரி.
: நீங்க(ள்) எந்த ஊர் ? :திருச்சி . நீங்க(ள்)? :நா(ன்) கன்னியாகுமரி.
ocabulary
எந்த எந்த which
Cultural note: Many of them in tamil, ask "you which city" to know the where the person is from. It's that easy! We will see the alternative version in the unit. But for now, let's keep it simple.
Well, can we revise all at once?
: எப்படி இருக்கிறீர்கள் : நான் நன்றாக இருக்கிறேன், நீங்கள்? : நானும் நன்றாக இருக்கிறேன்
: வணக்கம்! உங்கள் பெயர் என்ன ? : என் பெயர் அருண், உங்கள் பெயர் என்ன ? : என் பெயர் ராம் .
: நீங்கள் எந்த ஊர் ? :திருச்சி . நீங்கள்? :நான் கன்னியாகுமரி.
C.T: : எப்படி இருக்கீங்க : நா(ன்) நல்லா இருக்கே(ன்), நீங்க? : நானு(ம்) நல்லா இருக்கே(ன்)
: ஹலோ, உங்க பேரு என்ன : எ(ன்) பேரு அருண், உங்க பேரு? : எ(ன்) பேரு ராம்
: நீங்க(ள்) எந்த ஊர் ? :திருச்சி . நீங்க(ள்)? :நா(ன்) கன்னியாகுமரி.
Things to Remember: Verbs always placed at the end of the sentence
